meta.title: 4.4 V8 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Land Rover Range Rover Sport 4.4 V8 — real UK costs, timing chain guide insight, and honest reliability data for the AJ-V8 4.4. Get free quotes from 12 vetted Land Rover specialists today.

Land Rover Range Rover Sport 4.4 V8 Engine Codes

mainEntity.name: How much does a Land Rover Range Rover Sport 4.4 V8 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned 4.4 V8 engine typically costs £4,500–£6,500 supply only. Used engines are available from £2,500–£4,500. Fitting adds £1,300–£2,000 depending on your location and workshop. Most Range Rover Sport engine replacements range between £3,000 and £8,000. Main dealer new engines are no longer available — the 448PN has been out of production since 2009.

mainEntity.name: Is the Land Rover Range Rover Sport 4.4 V8 engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 4.4 AJ-V8 is widely regarded as one of the most reliable petrol engines ever fitted to a Range Rover Sport. It's a smooth, naturally aspirated V8 with no turbocharger complexity — a 'bulletproof' design when properly maintained. However, it has known weak points: plastic timing chain guides that can crack around 80,000–120,000 miles, cooling system leaks, and VVT phaser wear. Regular maintenance is essential.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Land Rover Range Rover Sport 4.4 V8?

acceptedAnswer.text: The most frequently reported issues are timing chain guide failure (plastic guides crack and fragment around 80,000–120,000 miles), cooling system leaks from the thermostat housing or water pump, and VVT phaser wear (often flagged by codes P0012 and P0022). The engine is all-aluminium, so overheating must be avoided.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Range Rover Sport 4.4 V8 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value. Against a well-kept £8,000 L320, a £5,500 recon is 69% of the car's value — approaching our 60% Land Rover threshold for petrol engines. Against a tired £4,000 example, the same recon exceeds the threshold significantly — get a scrap valuation first. However, well-maintained 4.4 V8 Range Rover Sports are becoming collectable.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Range Rover Sport 4.4 V8 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The 4.4 V8 carries the engine code 448PN (also referred to as AJ-V8 or AJ41 4.4 in some documentation). It's a 4.4-litre naturally aspirated V8 petrol engine used in the Range Rover Sport L320 from 2005 to 2009.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the timing chain guides, or does it need full engine replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — if caught early. A preventative timing chain guide and tensioner replacement costs £1,200–£2,500. The plastic guides crack and fragment predictably around 80,000–120,000 miles. If ignored and fragments circulate, they can block oil pickup and cause catastrophic engine failure. At the first sign of cold-start rattle lasting more than 3 seconds, book an inspection immediately. Regular oil changes using 5W-30 spec oil are the single most effective preventative measure.

replacementCosts.h2: Land Rover Range Rover Sport 4.4 V8 Engine Replacement Costs

rows.bestFor: No longer available from Land Rover

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the AJ-V8 4.4-litre petrol engine. Most Range Rover Sport engine replacements range between £3,000 and £8,000 depending on engine type and specification. Used engines are available from approximately £2,500–£4,500, with reconditioned units ranging from £4,500–£6,500. Main dealer new engines are no longer available — the 448PN has been out of production since 2009.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a Range Rover Sport 4.4 V8 engine typically takes 14–18 hours at £70–£100/hr (independent) or £120–£160/hr (London/SE dealer).

cta.label: → Compare 4.4 V8 engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Timing Chain Guide Failure
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise on cold start lasting 3+ seconds, metallic ticking from the engine, engine warning light, possible misfire.
problems.repairCost: £1,200–£2,500 (guide + tensioner replacement)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.replacementCost: £4,500–£7,500 (if chain skips and valves contact pistons)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattle lasting more than 3 seconds, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: The 4.4 AJ-V8's plastic timing chain guides are a known weak point — they can crack and fragment over time. This engine shares architecture with Jaguar's AJ133, and the plastic tensioner guides fail predictably around 80,000–120,000 miles. When fragments circulate, they can block oil pickup and cause catastrophic failure. At the first sign of cold-start rattle, get the guides and tensioners inspected immediately. Regular oil changes using the correct 5W-30 spec are essential.

failureLink.label: Read the full timing chain failure guide

problems.title: Cooling System Leaks
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss with no visible external leak, white exhaust smoke, overheating, sweet smell from the engine bay.
problems.recommendation: Coolant leaks from the thermostat housing or water pump are common on the 4.4 V8. The engine's all-aluminium construction means overheating can quickly lead to warped heads or head gasket failure. If you're experiencing unexplained coolant loss, have the cooling system inspected promptly.

failureLink.label: Read the full coolant leak guide

problems.title: VVT Phaser Wear
problems.symptoms: Rough idle, loss of power, poor fuel economy, engine warning light with VVT-related codes (P0012, P0022)
problems.recommendation: The 4.4 V8's variable valve timing system relies on VVT phasers that can wear over time. If ignored, failed phasers can cause timing issues and eventually contribute to timing chain problems. Regular oil changes using the correct 5W-30 specification are essential — these phasers are hydraulically actuated and sensitive to oil quality and pressure.

failureLink.label: Read the full VVT failure guide

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Range Rover Sport 4.4 V8 — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Preventative — catches the fault before catastrophic engine failure
Specialist repair available; cheaper than engine replacement
Labour-intensive; requires timing cover removal and oil system flush
Typically requires full engine replacement or complete rebuild

buyingChecks.title: Buying This 4.4 V8? Check These First
buyIf: * Documented timing chain guide inspection or replacement history
* Cooling system service records (thermostat housing, water pump replaced)
* Full service history with 5W-30 oil changes at 10,000-mile (or less) intervals
* No cold-start rattle — a quiet startup suggests healthy guides and tensioners
avoidIf: * Cold-start rattle lasting more than 3 seconds with no timing chain history
* Unexplained coolant loss with no cooling system service records
* Poor service history — this V8 is durable but unforgiving of neglect
* Engine warning light with VVT-related codes and no diagnosis

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The 4.4 V8 is one of the most reliable petrol engines ever fitted to a Range Rover Sport — a smooth, naturally aspirated V8 with none of the turbo or diesel complexity that plagues other variants. But it's an ageing engine now, and plastic timing chain guides and cooling system components are the price of entry. Buy on documented history, not on faith — a well-maintained 4.4 V8 L320 is a £5,000–£10,000 future classic; a neglected one is a £5,000 engine replacement waiting to happen.

cta.label: → Compare 4.4 V8 engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Land Rover Range Rover Sport 4.4 V8 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling lasting more than 3 seconds (timing chain guide wear warning)
* Service History — including the 4.4 V8 era (2005–2009) and confirmed oil-change intervals using 5W-30 spec oil
* Coolant Check — Any history of coolant loss or thermostat housing/water pump replacement?
* Oil Pressure — Check the oil pressure warning light; low pressure can indicate timing chain guide fragment blockages
* Smoke Check — White smoke on startup can indicate coolant ingress from cooling system failure
* VVT Operation — Any hesitation, rough idle, or loss of power? (VVT phaser wear warning)
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved timing, VVT (P0012/P0022), or cooling system issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures (cats, O2 sensors)
